title: IEEE-compliant force/release handling
body:
## Summary

This PR rewrites `V3Force`.

The previous implementation only provided partial `force/release` support and relied on a static model that does not match SystemVerilog semantics in the general case. In particular, it could not correctly handle cases where the active force depends on runtime control flow, and it did not scale to the broader set of LHS forms needed for full support.

This rewrite replaces the old per-object shadow-signal model with runtime force tracking based on `VlForceVec` together with `read()`, `readIndex()`, `addForce()`, and `release()` helpers.

## Motivation

The old implementation created a small set of shadow variables per forced signal:

- `__VforceEn`
- `__VforceVal`
- `__VforceRd`

and then rewrote reads to select between the original value and the forced value.

That worked for simple cases, but the model was fundamentally static. In practice, it tracked the last force site associated with a signal in the transformed AST, not the force that was actually active at runtime.

That breaks for cases such as:

- conditional `force`
- multiple `force` RHS values targeting the same LHS

In those cases, correctness depends on which `force` statements executed, not which ones happened to be seen last by the frontend. Extending the old implementation with more special cases would not fix that. The representation itself had to change.

Here is the most basic example for which it fails:
```verilog
`define IMPURE_ONE |($random | $random)
module top;
  wire a;
  reg b;
  initial begin
    if (`IMPURE_ONE == 1) force a = 1;
    if (`IMPURE_ONE == 0) force a = b;
    b = 0;
    #1 $display(a);
    release a;
  end
endmodule
```
Only the first force assignment will be executed during runtime, but the current implementation will just update with whatever was the last encountered `force`, resulting in assigning `0` to `a` when `b` changes to 0.

## Design

In the new implementation, force tracking happens at runtime rather than in rewritten AST expressions.

For each forced signal, Verilator now creates:

- a `VlForceVec` that stores the active forced ranges
- one shadow RHS storage object per force site
- force-aware reads using `read()` or `readIndex()`

During verilation:

- `force` lowers to `addForce(...)`
- `release` lowers to `release(...)`
- reads lower to `read(...)` calls

`VlForceVec` stores active entries as a sorted, non-overlapping set of ranges. Each entry contains:

- the destination range
- a pointer to the RHS shadow storage
- the RHS offset corresponding to destination index 0

When a new force overlaps an existing one, the existing entries are trimmed or removed so that the active set remains non-overlapping.

That means the result now depends on which of the forces actually executed, which is the behavior the old implementation could not model correctly.

## LHS handling

For ordinary bitwise objects, a force target is represented as a normal bit range and stored directly in `VlForceVec`.

For unpacked arrays, nested selections are flattened into a single logical element index. Reads use:

- `readIndex(...)` for element reads
- `read(...)` for whole-array reads

This avoids pretending unpacked array elements are packed slices of one flat object.

Some selected member / aggregate paths still do not lower cleanly to a meaningful packed `[lsb:msb]` range. For those cases, this PR uses a stable identity-based representation instead:

- the full path is converted to a key using `forcePathKey(...)`
- that key is assigned a synthetic index in `m_forcePathToIndex`
- that synthetic index is then used consistently for `force`, `release`, and readback

So instead of forcing everything into a packed-range model, paths that are semantically valid but not naturally expressible that way are handled as opaque logical locations.

## Release semantics

This also keeps release behavior correct.

When releasing a variable that is not continuously driven, Verilator first reads back the currently forced value and assigns it to the variable. That preserves the value until the next procedural assignment.

For continuously driven signals, `release` simply removes the force and exposes the underlying driver again.

## Earlier approaches tried and rejected

Two other approaches were implemented before landing on this one.

First, I tried expanding every read into a force-aware AST expression that merged all possible force sites. That fixed the semantic issue and passed the relevant tests, but compile-time and memory usage were not acceptable. In large designs, especially after `V3Width` flattening, the transformed AST became enormous.

A concrete example was Caliptra, where one transformed object ended up as a 13,923-bit vector with 22,586 `force` sites, 2,736 `release` sites, and 3,630 reads. That version could reach roughly 512 GB RAM in under two minutes during verilation.

The next attempt moved the merge logic into runtime loops in generated {--HDL/--}C++. That avoided AST blowup and was still semantically correct, but simulation performance was too slow. On the same Caliptra case, simulation could exceed 3 hours (vs under a minute normally).

So to summarise previous approaches:

- the original implementation was fast but incomplete
- AST expansion was correct but not scalable
- runtime merge loops were correct but too slow

This rewrite is the first version that is both correct and practical.

## Performance

On RTLMeter benchmarks, this implementation did not show meaningful regression in either full verilate/build time or simulation performance.

Across 10 runs per benchmark, using the median:

- full verilate + build time deltas ranged from **-0.98% to +0.75%**
- simulation time deltas ranged from **-1.55% to +0.52%**

Representative numbers:

- **NVDLA**
  - full build: 1429.61s -> 1427.39s
  - simulation: 222.26s -> 222.03s
- **OpenTitan**
  - full build: 270.86s -> 271.00s
  - simulation: 141.71s -> 142.45s
- **Vortex**
  - full build: 239.84s -> 237.48s
  - simulation: 38.41s -> 38.37s

RAM usage differences were also negligible.

So this closes the main semantic gaps without introducing a measurable performance problem on real designs.

## Follow-up work enabled by this

This rewrite also gives a cleaner base for follow-up work that did not make sense to stack into the same PR:

- full VPI support
- a cleaner DPI interface exposing `addForce()`, `release()`, and `read()` directly instead of the compatibility `__Vforce*` signals
- full `assign/deassign` support (fits naturally on top of the same machinery, since semantically it is a narrower version of `force/release`)
- support for function calls on the RHS of `force`


## Conclusion

The main change here is that `V3Force` no longer tries to resolve force state statically during rewriting. Force state is now tracked at runtime, which is what is needed to handle dynamic force selection correctly and to support more complicated LHS forms without blowing up either compile time or simulation time.
